articles.title: 100-Million-Year-Old Snake Fossil Rewrites Evolution Story
articles.summary: A fossil of the ancient snake Najash rionegrina, found in Argentina, reveals it had hind legs and a cheekbone that modern snakes lack. Scientists used micro-CT scanning to study the skull in detail. The discovery challenges the old idea that snakes evolved from small burrowers. Instead, evidence suggests early snakes were large-bodied and big-mouthed. Later studies added twists, including a 2020 blind snake fossil and a 2025 squamate fossil, showing snake evolution was complex.

articles.title: First Lava Tube Discovered on Venus — and It's Enormous
articles.summary: By reanalyzing 1990s radar data from NASA's Magellan probe, scientists discovered a massive lava tube on Venus near the volcano Nyx Mons. The tube is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide, larger than any on Earth or Mars. A collapsed skylight about 150 meters deep revealed its presence. This discovery will inform future missions like NASA's VERITAS and ESA's EnVision, which will carry advanced radar. The finding raises questions about how such large tubes form under Venus's Earth-like gravity.

articles.title: The Robot Therapist: How Maja Matarić Created Socially Assistive Robotics
articles.summary: Professor Maja Matarić pioneered socially assistive robotics, creating robots that provide therapy through social interaction. Her robots help children with autism spectrum disorder and students with anxiety. In 2025, she won the MassRobotics Robotics Medal. Matarić's journey from Belgrade to MIT and USC shows how she built a field from scratch. Her robot Bandit plays games and gives affirmations, while newer robots assist with cognitive behavioral therapy.
